sentences.text: So we were still with the Russians, huh?
So I had lost my mother again in-- in-- in [?
Czernowitz. ?] I-- I was very, very worried.
When we traveled through Russia, everything was bombed.
Everything was burned.
You know, it was very, very difficult situation.
I thought, if she is left alone, how on earth will she ever find us?
And we were sleeping on the floor.
We had blankets, our own blankets.
It was still my eiderdowns, which I carried with me everywhere.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one morning, I woke up and somebody was pushing me.
And there was my mother.
And after, I think, about a week, she had found me.
And she had a very interesting story to say.
She was left behind.
And she walked to the next station.
And there, they made people understand that she had lost her transport, and she didn't know where to-- so they told her there will be another transport going that direction and she can go in.
And that was a transport of Russian soldiers going back to Russia, who had come from the front.
And an officer took her in his sort of a jeep on the-- on the-- was a train with cars, with platforms and military vehicles.
And he took her in his jeep and said she can stay with him till they get off.
And the whole way, she had to drink vodka.
And, you know, when she said, no, no, he was very cross.
And she must drink vodka.
And he tried to speak to her and where she came from.
And she tried to explain him from Holland, water and all that.
And he understood, and he said, oh, Rembrandt, [INAUDIBLE].
And he seemed to know about paintings.
And he was-- he was an officer, so he was quite knowledgeable about things in the west.
And she said she had a very interesting time.
But by the time she had traveled a few hours, they were both so drunk that, you know, she didn't like it anymore.
And he started to want to kiss her and things.
And then at the next stop, he fell asleep.
And she didn't like it anymore and she got off.
And then, again, she had to tell how-- and this was still Poland then.
And they did understand.
Some were Germans.
And then one in the station there, it was all very primitive, you know?
I mean, not the station-- wasn't the same.
It was just a-- a little building.
And the sort of station master said, you should stay.
He was going to fetch somebody.
And he fetched a Jewish person who was living in a bombed-out under the ground.
And there was a little Jewish community where he took my mother and she spent the night there.
And it was-- they had two little rooms where about 10 people were sleeping under the table, on the table.
But, again, they're were very hospitable.
And they told her all kind of stories, what had happened in the war.
And-- and he said in the morning at 6:00 or still in the dark, they will be a train going.
And he will take her there.
And so--
In a little village on this road.
And so she had many stops and many changes.
Once she traveled with Italian prisoners of war who had been liberated.
And that was a proper compartment.
And so they spoke Italian and gave her, again, food and things.
And it was all very interesting, she said.
And one, once was-- was a British prisoner of war.
And when she wanted to change something on her clothes, he went outside.
She said, he was still a gentleman, you know?
It was quite typical.
They all just were all [INAUDIBLE] had forgotten that the man would go out if a woman wants to do, you know, something, like we were all quite open.
But said make a great impression on her.
And so somehow, she must have been able to get information where this transport from Auschwitz had gone.
And they knew it had gone to [?
Czernowitz. ?] And they put her, then, eventually on a train what was going to go there.
So and when she came to me, instead of cuddling her and kissing her, I was very close with her.
And I shouted at her, how could you have done that to me and to leave me again?
And, you know, I was very, very cross with her.
And she was very upset with me that I-- it just wasn't her fault.
So-- but anyway, we were reunited.
Then I said, and you must never leave my side anymore.
And so we went on and on east, southeast and till eventually, we ended up in Odessa.
And that's where they said they were going to leave us till the end of the war.
And then we can go back by boat to the West.
And they put us up in a beautiful old palace.
Nothing was in it, but you can still see lovely rooms.
Again, it was in a-- in a-- on the floor we had to sleep.
But it was a big park.
And in this park, all kind of groups had been put.
There were big group of Australian prisoners of war.
And they got, from the Red Cross they get parcels.
And so we got all kind of things then.
And they said, as well, we can send messages.
But these actually from Odessa never reached anybody, so I think it just never got further than Odessa.
And we had evening [INAUDIBLE], like prisoners or wars and made little theater acting, and-- because we were there for quite-- perhaps six weeks or perhaps even longer.
But the Russians had said we can do in the-- in the park whatever we want.
But we are not allowed to leave that.
But then they had miscalculated my mother's adventure spirit.
And she said, once we are in Odessa, we must see Odessa.
And once you are near the sea, you must swim in the sea, the Black Sea.
So we escaped from the Russians.
There was a guard.
We escaped through a hedge or something and went into town.
Of course, we couldn't speak, and we didn't know where to go.
It was all very tricky, you know, on a tram.
But we did all that.
We-- and we had great difficulty coming back because we said we'll look at that [INAUDIBLE], but it was in Russian, of course, the number and the text.
And we didn't know which time we had to take.
But nevertheless we went.
And somehow, we did find back.
How, I don't know again.
And we went-- there was big markets.
And we went, again, to-- to bargain there with whatever we had.
Always food, you always wanted still only food.
And I sold there.
I swapped my boots for a chicken or something.
[LAUGHS] So I lost them then.
And-- but we did get clothes then from the Russians.
They-- they measured us up for Russian uniforms, for the army uniforms, and-- and even for bras.
And, I mean, there was big tents where the had all those clothes.
And they gave us a check, I mean, the tape measure bit, but it wasn't so exact.
And so bosoms, they take fitted with their hands.
They felt, you know?
And then they went [INAUDIBLE].
I think this will do, and, you know, but all laughing and drinking, what conversate.
And no-- not dirty, you know?
It was all good fun.
But, again, some of other people said, oh, shocking.
How can they behave like that, you know?
But I thought it was all very funny.
And one day when we arrived back, we got caught.
And they were very, very cross with us.
And they took us at the entrance there was sort of a dog kennel.
And they said, you behaved like a dog, not like a-- like a human being.
You'll go into this dog kennel.
And they put us in there, and locked up.
And that you spent the night there.
And I was very hungry.
And the other inmates came and bought us some bits of food and so on.
And-- but, you know, so I did it.
But, I mean, it was actually very naughty of them.
But we didn't take it as a-- degradation or so, you know?
I said, they have told us we shouldn't go out.
And if we did it, well, they did punish us, you know?
We didn't really mind so much that they did that.
So we were quite upset, really.
So-- but those things they did.
Was that the bad incident that you referred to earlier?
Yes, that's right, you know?
I mean, some people could say, shocking to put you in a dig kennel.
But that's how they were.
They were childlike, you know?
They thought it was a big joke, really.
And, well, and my mother made friends with the Australians-- and-- because she could speak English.
And we had quite an enjoyable time really there.
And Otto Frank was there, as well.
But we didn't really see him in Odessa.
I had met him once when we had gone out, you know, when we were walking next to the railway track and waiting for the train to move on.
I saw him, and I introduced him to my mother.
I said, this is Mr.
Frank, who-- my mother knew Anne.
But she hadn't-- she didn't remember that she knew Otto.
And that's Anne's father, and so I got talking a bit.
And-- but in-- in-- Odessa, in this place we never saw him because it was very big.
As I say, there were many different groups of people.
And then the-- yeah, and then the end of the war, that was then May.
And one day they came and said, the war is finished, just like that.
And--
Well, that was, of course, you know, we started to dance, and to sing, and to kiss each-- everybody, and fantastic.
And the Russians said, to celebrate that, they're going to bring a dance and opera company into the big hall.
Like I said, it was a palace.
And there's a huge hall.
There's nothing in it, of course.
And so they erected sort of a stage and called everybody in.
We all sat on the floor.
And there came the most magnificent costumes and singers and dancing and big party with drink.
And they made a bit buffet with food.
Then-- and fireworks.
And it was a wonderful party.
And then they said, well, now, you have to go home.
So, of course, we're all dying to go home.
But they said, well, it will still take a little while because, you know, we have to wait for ships to come to take you.
But they registered everybody then.
I think that the Red Cross English people, English-speaking people came in and registered where we want to go, where we had come from.
And they were going to organize it.
And there was one Australian who got very friendly with my mother.
And he said, look, why do you want to go back to Holland?
You have [INAUDIBLE] this terrible war.
And what you want to do?
I am a sheep farmer.
I've got lots of land and lots of sheep.
I'll marry you, and you can come with me to Australia.
And your daughter can come too.
And, you know, I'll be very good for you.
And my mother said, no, but I've got my husband and my son now to go, you know.
And he tried to convince her.
But of course, she wouldn't want to go.
And later, he still go to us.
We got a letter once from him.
Said he married and he was quite happy.
And that was-- [LAUGHS] that was a very early offer of marriage.
[LAUGHS] And, I mean, you know, my mother still looked pretty bad.
And as I say, it was just stress and a shaved-- [INAUDIBLE] hair had started to grow a bit.
But we were still quite skeletal.
And after a little while, a big troop transport, a New Zealand troop transport ship, the came.
And everybody who was going direction Marseilles, it was going to Marseilles, so French prisoners of wars, and all Dutch, Belgium, French people, all were going on this boat.
And the men were in the hold, but the women were in cabins.
We were four to a cabin.
There was a Dutch girl called [?
Kaya, ?] who I had met in Odessa.
She was coming in with us.
The fourth, I can't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I think it was-- that's another story, but, I mean, it's not my story.
It's a [INAUDIBLE].
It's another woman who had come all the way from us-- with us from-- well, first from Holland, but then from Auschwitz, had done all this [INAUDIBLE].
And she was similar age to my mother.
And she has had a daughter, who she had lost somewhere in Auschwitz, was separated and didn't know.
And she was always very sad.
And she said later on she was very jealous of-- that my mother was with me all the time.
But she-- she joined with us.
And so there were four of us.
And we had still our Russian uniforms, but from the English or the New Zealanders, we got plimsolls to walk on deck.
And we-- well, we had it very good.
We could sit outside.
It was June.
It was lovely, you know, lovely weather.
And it was a fantastic journey through-- I mean, it's a luxury cruise that goes through from Odessa, through the Dardanelles, and the [INAUDIBLE], and Turkey, and then Greece, and Italy.
And it was wonderful.
And the first meal we had, we-- they called us to the dining room.
And there was white table cloths, and silver-plated cutlery, and glasses, and wonderful, you know?
The-- and we had sheets.
It was just-- it was-- even with the Russian, we had floor, you know?
And this was the first time we had bed with proper things and proper food and proper everything.
And we always got a roll next to the food.
And this roll, we always pocketed.
Oh, and then we got more rolls, and we always pocketed.
And we always took all this to the cabin.
What we couldn't eat, we took to the cabin.
And one day, the captain made an announcement to ask to not to take all this food to the cabin.
It gets moldy, and we might get mice.
We shouldn't worry.
There's enough food.
And whenever we want more, we can just ask.
There's plenty.
So don't take this food with you.
But it is a habit which was difficult for me to lose, even now.
I mean, the last few years, actually, I've stopped doing it.
But whenever in a hotel, I always take food, used to take food back to the room or whatever.
And that's something the children used to be rather ashamed of me when I did that.
But it's just something, my mother too, I just couldn't leave alone.
So we had this really nice journey.
And then in Turkey, the consuls in Istanbul, the consuls came.
And first they said, we can go on land.
We can go out.
But then the Turks didn't want us because they said we might bring illnesses in.
But the consuls came on board.
And that's-- they said they will-- we can give some letters to post.
And-- and this is the first letter my grandparents got from us.
And I think my mother still got this letter where we said, we have survived.
We've been in concentration camp.
And we're on the way back to Holland where we hope we will find [INAUDIBLE] and Heinz.
So-- and then we went to somewhere, to Naples where we dropped off some of the Italian prisoners of war.
And then we went to Marseilles.
And there was the most fantastic welcome there.
There was a band of music and flags and, well, a military band that was wonderful.
And everybody-- when everybody walked down the gangplank, they were clapping and playing some more drums.
And-- and it was really, you know, we were quite taken with all that.
And then there was a big-- lots of tents.
And everything was, again, registered.
And we got, again, different clothes and-- and food and wine and really the French way, you know?
And then-- then my mother volunteered again to be a translator.
And, again, I was cross, because we were taken somewhere to a big hall where we had this lovely banquet.
And my mother missed that.
And I was very cross with her.
I said, always you have to go off.
And now you missed all this.
And anyway, [LAUGHS] but she always-- she was a great busybody at that time.
So when, as a married woman, she was very subdued.
My father was very strong.
And she was always in the background.
But after what, she became very, very strong.
I the camp must have taught her that you have to push yourself, your way through things.
Anyway, so and then I think by the evening they said, everybody who is the French, who had to go that way, stayed, of course.
And we boarded a train.
And we're going on to Holland.
And each little town we stopped, and at each town there was a welcoming committee, even in the night.
Music and wine was brought in.
And everywhere there were shouting and singing, and the-- and at some stations, there were gallows erected and Hitler hanging.
And later, we heard that the welcoming was not really meant for us.
It was really meant for the French prisoners of war, because at each stop there were, you know?
And that was-- they didn't really know about Auschwitz survivor or people that go by going on to Belgium or Holland.
But anyway, we enjoyed it, just the same.
And so that took a while, and a few days, I think, because we just were-- we are broken and the railway line.
It was all still very disorganized.
And then we went to Belgium.
And then we went to the south of Holland, Limburg and [?
Heuvelland, ?] where we had to stop because there was a-- they give us-- there were no bridges yet across the rivers into the North Holland.
And we were put into sort of a monastery.
And in Holland, the welcome was not particular attractive.
They took very little notice of us.
And there were different transport coming from different parts.
And [SIGHS] we were sort of neglected.
And we-- there we felt a bit cross, so to say, you know?
We thought, now we come to our own country.
And, I mean, we were not feeling that Dutch.
But there were real Dutch people, and they felt quite shocked that they were not received.
Later, again, we heard that the war had, in France, it was already long [INAUDIBLE] liberated.
But in Holland, it was very short.
They were liberated.
And the last winter was terrible.
They had really no food.
Whatever food they produced was sent to-- to Germany.
And it was a very cold winter.
They had no way of heating.
So they burnt lots of doors, and they burnt furniture, and they eat-- ate even bulbs, tulip bulbs and grass and whatever.
So they were not prepared for more mouths to feed and to make it easy now, because they were really organizing themself.
And it was, indeed, it was-- when we got into Amsterdam, there was really nothing.
But we didn't realize at first.
But one wonderful thing happened one day.
Another transport came from somewhere.
And on this transport was the daughter of this [INAUDIBLE], who was similar my age.
And she had been evacuated or what had gone on those terrible transports from Auschwitz to another camp.
It might have been even Bergen-Belsen, but I don't think so.
No, I don't think so.
Otherwise, she wouldn't have ended up in [?
Heuvelland. ?] Anyway, she was liberated somewhere and sent there.
And she found her mother again.
And that was, of course, you know, wonderful.
And-- and we, of course, all hoped that we would-- that Heinz and my father might come as well there.
So, you know, every day we waited.
And that, I think, that was where we were kind of not so happy there, because we were very anxious.
And we were very anxious to move on, not to stay there.
But, as I say, we couldn't, because there was no way of transport.
And eventually, I think we went on a bus then.
Slowly, we made progress up to Amsterdam, first, by bus, and then by train.
And so we arrived in Amsterdam Central Station.
And we expected a welcoming committee.
And there was nothing.
We got off the train.
We had our-- what-- I had my rucksack, and my blanket, and a few other things, not a penny, no money, no nothing.
And so [SIGHS] we didn't know how-- how and what.
We didn't know what had happened to our flat or anything.
So we went to a family called Rosenberg, which was a man who used to live in-- he didn't live on [INAUDIBLE], but just around the corner.
And he was Jewish, but the wife was a Christian Austrian.
And-- so mixed marriages were not always safe.
In this case, they were safe.
Nothing happened to him.
And so we decided we would go-- we couldn't think of anybody where we would go.
We decided we would go to him, hoping that he would still be there.
So we rode in the tram without money.
We said we had no money.
And we just, I mean, you know?
And, of course, they did let us.
But it was very-- well, it was a very sad coming back to your town, because we thought we'll be helped or anything.
There was nothing, nothing at all.
So we went to this house.
And we rang the bell, knocked, knocked because the bell didn't work.
And then Mr.
Rosenberg opens the door and didn't-- had no idea who we were at first, didn't expect us to come.
And then we told him.
And [?
Elfriede ?] Geiringer and Eva.
Ah, ah, ah.
Come, come, come.
And ah, and couldn't believe [INAUDIBLE] because we had disappeared for three years in '42.
He didn't know where we were.
And where is Erich and Heinz?
We don't know.
And then where your wife?
Oh, she's just in hospital having her first baby.
And she was an older woman.
And he was very-- and he was very old, actually.
And he was very excited that he was going to have a child.
And so he said, of course, you can stay here.
My wife isn't here anyway.
So you can look after me.
You can run the household.
So we said yes.
And-- but as I said, there was nothing.
He had a little paraffin stove where there was a little bit [SIGHS] to cook.
But there was nothing to buy in the shop.
And it was really, very, very, very hard, even then.
So we stayed there and, again, I'm not sure why we didn't go to our flat.
But we didn't at first.
And then after a little while, we said we're going to look there to the flat.
And we went there.
And this lady was indeed there.
And everything was left how we had left it.
She lived there, and it was-- so we went in.
And that was very, very weird, you know, after three years to come back.
And my father had made on the door marks how we had grown when we-- and, you know, the marks on our room were still there.
And it-- it was-- it was eerie, you know, to-- after I'd had come out hell, suddenly, to find everything untouched.
It was unreal.
So-- and this lady said, well, if you want to come back, of course, you know, she had her own room again.
And she could go back.
And so we said, yes.
We'd like to.
So we stayed a little bit at the Rosenbergs to get her a chance to go back.
And, as well, she came home with the baby, so we helped there a bit.
And then we moved back.
